MC: What does a class object's __dict__ contain?

column3: The names and values assigned at the top level of the class body
Only the names assigned to instances created from the class
A list of all superclasses in the class tree
Only the names explicitly exported with __all__

MC: In the demo, id(Animal) is printed before any instances are created. What does this confirm?

column3: That Animal is stored in the same location as its instances
That Animal's id changes every time an instance is created
That a class object exists in memory before any instances are made from it
That id() cannot be used on class objects

MC: A student creates two instances: a1 = Animal() and a2 = Animal(), then assigns a1.name = 'Rex' and a2.name = 'Luna'. Which statement is true about their __dict__ attributes?

column3: Both instances share the same __dict__ because they come from the same class
a1.__dict__ contains name and speak; a2.__dict__ contains only name
Neither instance has a __dict__; all attributes live in the class
Each instance has its own __dict__ containing only the names assigned directly to it

MC: When Python resolves an attribute reference on an instance, where does it look first?

column3: The instance's own __dict__
The class's __dict__
The object built-in class
The module where the class is defined

MC: What does __mro__ stand for and what does it describe?

column3: Method Return Object; the value returned by each method in the class
Module Resolution Order; the order in which Python searches imported modules
Method Resolution Order; the sequence Python follows when searching for an attribute in a class tree
Method Registration Object; a registry of all methods defined in a class

MC: What class sits at the top of every Python class tree?

column3: type
object
Animal
base

MC: In the demo, __repr__ is searched for in Animal.__mro__ and found in object not in Animal. What does this demonstrate?

column3: That __repr__ must be explicitly imported before it can be used
That Animal has no string representation unless one is defined
That object overrides any __repr__ defined in subclasses
That classes inherit default behaviors from object when they do not define their own
Only when explicitly called by the programmer
Once when the class is defined not when instances are created
Only when the instance is printed or converted to a string
Automatically each time an instance is created from the class

MC: What is self in a method definition?

column3: The first argument of a method filled in automatically by Python with the instance the method is called on
A keyword that Python reserves for the class itself
A reference to the class object not the instance
A copy of the instance created at the start of each method call

MC: In the demo, id(dog) is printed outside a method and id(self) is printed inside a method called on dog. The two values are identical. What does this confirm?

column3: That id() always returns the same value for objects of the same class
That self and dog are two separate copies of the same data
That self inside the method is the same object as dog outside — Python passes the instance automatically
That id() returns the name of the variable not a memory address

MC: cat = Animal('Whiskers', 'Cat', 4) and rabbit = Animal('Biscuit', 'Rabbit', 2) are both instances of Animal. When str(cat) and str(rabbit) are called they produce different output even though the same __str__ method runs for both. Why?

column3: Python selects a different version of __str__ based on the arguments passed to Animal()
self is different for each call so self.name resolves to a different value each time
Each instance has its own copy of __str__ with different code
__str__ runs twice — once for the class and once for the instance
Passing an instance to print() or str()
Calling the instance like a function
Assigning a new value to an instance attribute
Importing the module that contains the class

MC: What is the difference between __init__ and a regular method like speak()?

column3: __init__ can only be defined once per class; speak() can be defined multiple times
__init__ does not take self as its first argument; regular methods do
__init__ stores data permanently on the instance; regular methods only process it temporarily
__init__ is called automatically by Python at instance creation; speak() is called explicitly by name

MC: What is the name self in a method definition?

column3: A reserved keyword that Python enforces
A special variable automatically created by Python that cannot be renamed
A convention — any name could be used in its position but self is the strong standard
An alias for the class object

MC: How does a Python class declaration specify that a class inherits from another?

column3: Using the extends keyword: class Dog extends Animal
By listing the parent class in parentheses: class Dog(Animal)
Using an @inherit decorator above the class definition
Using a super statement inside the class body

MC: What does a subclass receive automatically from its parent without any additional code?

column3: Only the methods that begin with double underscores
Only the attributes assigned in __init__
Only methods that are explicitly listed in the parent's __all__
All attributes and methods defined in the parent class

MC: When a subclass defines a method with the same name as a method in the parent class what happens?

column3: The subclass version overrides the parent version for instances of the subclass
Python runs both methods and combines their return values
The parent version takes precedence because it was defined first
Python raises a NameError because two methods cannot share the same name

MC: Dog defines its own speak() method. Animal also defines speak(). When buddy.speak() is called on a Dog instance, why does Python use Dog.speak() rather than Animal.speak()?

column3: Because Dog.speak() is explicitly registered as the default
Because the MRO search starts at the instance and moves upward finding Dog before Animal
Because Animal.speak() is automatically disabled when a subclass is created
Because Dog is further from object in the class tree

MC: Dog does not define __str__. When str(buddy) is called on a Dog instance, where does Python find __str__?

column3: Python generates a default string from the instance's memory address
Python raises an AttributeError because Dog has no __str__
Python finds it in Animal the next class up the MRO from Dog
Python finds it in Dog.__dict__ because all inherited methods are copied there

MC: The demo loops over a list containing both Animal and Dog instances and calls speak() on each. Animal instances produce one result and Dog instances produce another. What does this demonstrate?

column3: Polymorphism: the same method call produces different behavior depending on the actual type of the object
Overloading: Python selects the correct method based on the number of arguments
Encapsulation: each object hides its internal data from the loop
Abstraction: speak() is hidden inside the class so it cannot be called from outside
A list of all methods defined directly in Dog
A dictionary mapping method names to their implementations
The list of instances currently created from Dog
The sequence of classes Python searches when looking up an attribute on a Dog instance
The same entries as Animal.__dict__ plus the names defined in Dog
All names accessible on a Dog instance, including those inherited from Animal
Only the names defined directly in Dog — speak and fetch — not inherited names
Only the instance attributes assigned in __init__

MC: What is the maintenance problem with rewriting __init__ from scratch in a subclass rather than using super()?

column3: The subclass __init__ runs before the parent __init__ causing attribute errors
Every attribute from the parent __init__ must be duplicated, so if the parent changes, the subclass must be updated separately
Python raises a TypeError when __init__ is defined in more than one class
The subclass cannot access any attributes set by the parent __init__

MC: What does super() do inside a method?

column3: It copies all attributes from the parent class into the current instance
It creates a new instance of the parent class
It returns an object that routes attribute lookups to the next class in the MRO allowing the subclass to call the parent's version of a method
It prevents the subclass from overriding the parent method

MC: ServiceDog inherits from Dog, and Dog inherits from Animal. Dog defines no __init__. When super().__init__(...) is called inside ServiceDog.__init__, which class's __init__ actually runs?

column3: Animal.__init__ because Dog has no __init__ so the MRO search continues to Animal
ServiceDog.__init__ because super() calls the current class
object.__init__ because object is the root of the tree
Dog.__init__ because Dog is the immediate parent of ServiceDog

MC: In ServiceDog.__init__, the call is super().__init__(name, species, age) followed by self.task = task. What does this division represent?

column3: super().__init__() runs the parent's validation; self.task stores the result
super().__init__() handles display; self.task handles storage
super().__init__() creates the instance; self.task initializes it
super().__init__() sets up the attributes shared with Animal; self.task adds only what is new to ServiceDog

MC: ServiceDog.__str__ is written as: base = super().__str__() then return f'{base} [Service: {self.task}]'. What does this pattern accomplish?

column3: It calls __str__ twice and concatenates both results
It reuses the parent's string output as a foundation and adds the service task on top
It replaces the parent __str__ entirely with a new implementation
It prevents Animal.__str__ from running when a ServiceDog is printed

MC: ServiceDog does not define fetch(), but apollo.fetch('harness') works correctly. What does this demonstrate?

column3: Composition: ServiceDog contains a Dog object that provides fetch()
Polymorphism: Python selects fetch() based on the argument type
Inheritance: ServiceDog inherits fetch() from Dog through the class tree without needing to define it
Overriding: ServiceDog silently overrides fetch() with a default version

MC: ServiceDog.__mro__ shows: [ServiceDog, Dog, Animal, object]. What does this sequence tell you about how Python resolves attributes for a ServiceDog instance?

column3: Python searches in the order shown — ServiceDog first, then Dog, then Animal, then object — stopping at the first match found
Python searches object first because it is the most general class
Python searches all four classes simultaneously and uses the most specific match
Python searches Animal first because it defines the most attributes
